figures.caption: Figure 1: Left: absolute power gain over standard procedures at 𝜃 = −2·0 as a function of 𝐾 . Right: computation cost per outer iteration of Algorithm 1, confirming polynomial scaling. Timing is on a single CPU core with 𝑁 = 100,000 Monte Carlo samples. Alt text: Two-panel line chart. Left panel shows the power gain of the algorithm over Bonferroni, Holm and Hommel, increasing with K from 3 to 12. The right panel shows per-iteration cost increasing polynomially with K
Table 4: Rejection decisions for six borderline replications from Camerer et al. [2018], 𝛼 = 0·05. Standard methods reject only the two smallest 𝑝-values; the optimal algorithm rejects all six

Table 1: Average power Π𝐾 and maximum empirical family-wise error rate for the truncated normal alternative at 𝜃 = −2·0, 𝛼 = 0·05. Monte Carlo standard errors for power estimates are approximately 0·002; for FWER estimates, approximately 0·001. Values in parentheses are 95% confidence intervals for the FWER. The optimal algorithm maintains nearly flat power as 𝐾 grows, while standard procedures lose power steadily.

Figure 4: Convergence of Algorithm 1: ∥𝜇 (𝑡 ) − 𝜇 (𝑡−1) ∥2 versus outer iteration 𝑡 on a logarithmic scale, for several 𝐾 values under the truncated normal model at 𝜃 = −2·0. The approximately linear decrease confirms geometric convergence with empirical contraction ratios between 0·3 and 0·7. Alt text: Log-scale line chart showing the norm of successive iterate differences versus outer iteration for K equals 3, 4, 6, 8 and 12. All curves decrease approximately linearly on the log scale before levelling off

Figure 2: Power curves for 𝐾=3 across four distributional models. Top left: truncated normal. Top right: mixture normal. Bottom left: Student-𝑡. Bottom right: beta. Left panels within each subplot show Π3; right panels show Πany. The algorithm (solid line) dominates all baselines across the signal range. Alt text: Four two-panel plots showing power versus signal strength for K equals 3. In each plot, the algorithm curve lies above Bonferroni, Holm, Hochberg and Hommel curves across all signal values

Figure 3: Power curves for 𝐾=6 across four distributional models. Comparing with Fig. 2, the gap between the algorithm and the baselines has widened at every signal strength, consistent with the scaling phenomenon in Table 1. Alt text: Four two-panel plots showing power versus signal strength for K equals 6. The gap between the algorithm curve and the baseline curves is wider than in the corresponding K equals 3 plots

Table 5: Sensitivity of the Camerer et al. result to the alternative density 𝑔. Standard methods reject 2 regardless of 𝑔. The algorithm rejects all 6 for every beta specification and for mild truncated normals; stronger truncated normals (𝜃 ≤ −2·0) yield 𝑙∗ = 3
